variables.content: In finite periodic simulation cells using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions (where the Jastrow and backflow factors each contain variational parameters), reoptimizing the Jastrow and backflow parameters for an excited-state configuration (an excitation produced by adding or removing an electron in a particular Bloch state) can lower the total DMC energy in that finite cell—for example, removing an electron from 𝗸=0 in a 26-electron paramagnetic 2D HEG at density parameter r_s=1 reduced the DMC total energy by 0.000854(4) hartree atomic units (a.u.)—but this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and tends to reduce the computed excitation bandwidth and thus amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass; therefore, to avoid introducing extra finite-size bias the Jastrow and backflow parameters are optimized in the ground state and then reused unchanged for excited-state energy evaluations.

Reoptimization of the explicit correlation parameters in finite-cell excited-state trial wave functions produces a measurable lowering of the DMC total energy of that finite cell and thereby increases the finite-size bias of excitation energies computed as total-energy differences. Concretely, when the Jastrow-factor and backflow-function parameters were reoptimized after removing an electron from wavevector \\(\\mathbf{k}=0\\) in a paramagnetic 26-electron 2D HEG at density parameter \\(r_{s}=1\\), the DMC total energy was lowered by 0.000854(4) atomic units (a.u.), which reduced the finite-cell bandwidth and therefore increased the finite-size error in the derived excitation energies. Consequently, to avoid introducing this additional finite-cell bias into excitation-energy estimates, the Jastrow and backflow parameters were optimized once in the closed-shell ground state for each density and system size and then held fixed for all corresponding excited-state DMC calculations.

For the three-electron molecular ion He$_2^+$ at fixed bond distance $R=2.0625$ bohr in its ground $^2\Sigma_{\text{u}}^+$ state, the variance-minimized explicitly correlated exponential expansions produce optimized VMC energies with representative values $E_{L=1}=-4.98078$ hartree, $E_{L=12}=-4.99400$ hartree, and $E_{L=23}=-4.99436$ hartree, relative to a Hartree–Fock reference value $E_{\text{HF}}=-4.92270$ hartree; a single-term expansion recovers about $80\%$ of the correlation energy relative to that Hartree–Fock limit, and a 12-term expansion already attains an energy lower than a reported literature CI value $-4.99389$ hartree. Fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) simulations using these optimized exponential trial functions (with the authors' statement that reported DMC numbers are after elimination of time-step bias) produce DMC energies that improve with the number of terms: reported DMC results are DMC$_{1}=-4.99422$ hartree, DMC$_{4}=-4.99435$ hartree, and DMC$_{8}=-4.99455$ hartree, and the 8-term DMC result is numerically similar to high-accuracy literature values such as $-4.994598$ hartree obtained with a 320-term stochastic exponentially correlated shifted Gaussian (SECSG) calculation.

For the $1^{1}B_{u}$ excited state of trans-hexatriene (C6H8), simultaneous optimization of orbitals and configuration-interaction (CI) coefficients was performed with large parameter sets (604 parameters optimized for the ground state and 493 parameters optimized for the excited state). The fully optimized excited-state variational Monte Carlo and diffusion Monte Carlo energies are reported as $E_{\mathrm{VMC}}=-38.495(1)$ hartree and $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}=-38.6069(8)$ hartree (parentheses denote one-standard-error sampling uncertainties), yielding a DMC vertical excitation energy $\Delta E=5.23(3)\,\text{eV}$ relative to the correspondingly optimized ground state; this DMC excitation energy agrees with the experiment-derived vertical excitation energy of $5.22\,\text{eV}$. By contrast, a reference CASSCF(2,2) wave function (two electrons in two orbitals, unoptimized) yields $\Delta E=5.63(3)\,\text{eV}$, and an unoptimized CAS(6,6) wave function with the leading five CSFs retained gives markedly worse VMC and DMC energies ($E_{\mathrm{VMC}}=-38.438(1)$ and $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}=-38.560(2)$ hartree), demonstrating that reoptimization of both determinant orbitals and CI linear coefficients is essential to recover accurate excitation energies for this system.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For a finite interacting HEG, denote the occupied-state occupation energies by \mathcal{E}_k for crystal-momentum states k with |k|<k_F. Fitting the computed \mathcal{E}_k over the occupied k range to the parabolic dispersion model
      $$
      \mathcal{E}_k \approx \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m^*}
      $$
      yields an effective mass parameter m^*; in the reported fit to DMC data for r_s=3.0 the fitted value is m^*=0.84\,m_e. This procedure assumes the occupied-state dispersion is well approximated by a parabola over the fitted range and that constant background energy shifts and finite-size discretization have been properly accounted for in the fit.

For the two-electron systems the DMC values of the combined mass-velocity and electron–nucleus Darwin contribution $\varepsilon_{2}+\varepsilon_{3}$ (in hartree) are: He $-0.000115$ (exact $-0.0001143$), $Be^{2+}$ $-0.00248$ (exact $-0.00243$), and $Ne^{8+}$ $-0.116$ (exact $-0.1158$). The close numerical agreement indicates that when the exact nonrelativistic nodal structure is available the DMC evaluation of the dominant mass-velocity and electron–nucleus Darwin terms reproduces essentially exact values.

For Jastrow-CIPSI trial functions at fixed optimized geometries obtained with a two-body Jastrow factor, augmenting the Jastrow factor to include explicit electron–electron–nucleus three-body terms and fully reoptimizing all wave-function parameters produces a measurable VMC energy lowering but leaves DMC total energies unchanged within the reported Monte Carlo statistical uncertainties: specifically, for N=2,048 determinants the reported change in VMC energy upon adding three-body terms is ΔE_VMC=−0.00522(29) hartree and the reported change in DMC energy is ΔE_DMC=−0.00015(26) hartree; for N=5,114 determinants ΔE_VMC=−0.00353(33) hartree and ΔE_DMC=+0.00012(18) hartree. These observations indicate that, for the high-quality CIPSI determinantal components considered here, the two-body Jastrow factor already recovers the bulk of dynamical correlation relevant to the nodal quality governing fixed-node DMC energies in these tests.

The zero-time-step, twist-averaged diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) ground-state energies per electron for the infinite paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) at the reported density parameters are: $E(r_{s}=1)= -0.2104(6)$ a.u. per electron, $E(r_{s}=5)= -0.14963(3)$ a.u. per electron, and $E(r_{s}=10)= -0.085399(6)$ a.u. per electron; here $r_{s}$ is the radius (in Hartree atomic units) of the circle containing one electron on average, energies are given in Hartree atomic units, the DMC calculations used Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions to define the fixed-node constraint, twist averaging where indicated to reduce single-particle finite-size effects, and the reported values have been extrapolated to infinite system size.

Let J_2 denote the two-body Jastrow factor including electron–nucleus and electron–electron polynomial terms enforcing cusp conditions, and J_3 denote the augmented Jastrow factor that additionally includes explicit electron–electron–nucleus three-body terms. For the Jastrow-CIPSI trial functions considered at determinant counts N=2,048 and N=5,114 on the fixed average geometries used in the tests, the reported data show that replacing J_2 with J_3 and fully reoptimizing the wave-function parameters yields VMC energy improvements of order a few×10^{-3} hartree (ΔE_VMC=−0.00522(29) hartree at N=2,048 and ΔE_VMC=−0.00353(33) hartree at N=5,114) while producing changes in fixed-node DMC energies that are within the reported Monte Carlo statistical uncertainties (ΔE_DMC=−0.00015(26) hartree at N=2,048 and ΔE_DMC=+0.00012(18) hartree at N=5,114), implying that J_2 recovers the bulk of dynamical correlation affecting nodal quality in these tested cases.
